coredata.dc:title: Theranostic lyotropic liquid crystalline nanostructures for selective breast cancer imaging and therapy

coredata.dc:description:
Herein, we report the development of theranostic lyotropic liquid crystalline nanostructures (LCN's) loaded with unique MnO nanoparticles (MNPs) for selective cancer imaging and therapy. MNPs serves as a fluorescent agent as well as a source of manganese (Mn2+) and enables localized oxidative stress under the hallmarks of cancer (acidosis, high H2O2 level). In pursuit of synergistic amplification of Mn2+ antitumor activity, betulinic acid (BA) is loaded in LCN's. In this investigation, nano-architecture of LCN's phase interface is established via SAXS, Cryo-TEM and Cryo-FESEM. Intriguing in vitro studies showed that the LCN's triggered hydroxyl radical production and exhibited greater selective cytotoxicity in cancer cells, ensuring the safety of normal cells. Significant tumor ablation is realized by the 96.5 % of tumor growth inhibition index of LCN's as compared to control group. Key insights into on-site drug release, local anti-cancer response, and tumor location are gained through precise guidance of fluorescent MNPs. In addition, comprehensive assessment of the safety, pharmacokinetics and tumor distribution behavior of LCN's is performed in vivo or ex vivo. This work emphasizes the promise of modulating tumor microenvironment with smart endogenous stimuli sensitive nano systems to achieve advanced comprehensive cancer nano-theranostics without any external stimulus.
Statement of significance Effective diagnosis and treatment approaches with maximum anti-cancer activity and minimal side-effects are critical to ameliorate cancer therapy. Compared to radiation, photodynamic and photothermal therapy, the specific and selective activation of tumor microenvironmental endogenous stimuli for the logical generation of cytotoxic OH· free radicals serves as an efficient therapeutic strategy for chemodynamic-cancer treatment. In this investigation, MnO nanoparticles fulfill two needs (fluorescence-based optical imaging and a source of Mn2+ based chemodynamic therapy) in one unit. This approach also ensures the safety of normal cells, as the toxic OH· free radical activity is substantially suppressed under the mild alkaline/H2O2 conditions in normal cell microenvironment.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: A) Illustration of step-by-step synthesis of Mn-oleate (MO) complex and MnO nanoparticles (MNPs); B) Photographs of synthesized MO complex and MNPs; C) Characterization of oleic acid (OA), MO and MNPs by Fourier transformed infrared spectroscopy (FTIR); D) Proton nuclear magnetic resonance spectroscopy (1H-NMR) spectra of OA, MO, MNP in CDCl3.

A, B) Cryogenic field emission scanning electron microscopic (Cryo-FESEM) images of LCN@Mn and LCN@Mn-BA respectively; C,D) Cryogenic transmission electron microscopy (CryoTEM) images of LCN@Mn and LCN@Mn-BA respectively; E,F) Particle size distribution of LCN@Mn and LCN@Mn-BA obtained through quasi-elastic light scattering experiment (QELS) respectively; Small-angle X-ray Scattering analysis (SAXS) depicting the inverse hexagonal (HII) phase of I) LCN@Mn and J) LCN@Mn-BA nanoparticle; G) Fluorescent image of blank LCN's without MNPs and H) Fluorescent images of LCN@Mn-BA nanoparticles at a wavelength of 633 nm; K) Presence of Mn and O elements in LCN@Mn-BA was confirmed by energy dispersive X-ray spectrometer (EDS) analysis; L) High resolution X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (HR-XPS) scans of Mn2p regions of MNPs loaded in final formulation (LCN@Mn-BA). Scale bar of images: 50 nm, 100 nm, 1 µm and 200 µm.

A) Schematic illustration of i) Fenton like reaction between manganese(II) and hydrogen peroxide generate highly reactive hydroxyl (OH•) radicals that cause oxidative stress in cancer cells. Disodium terephthalate, a sensitive probe for OH• radicals was used to detect and to confirm the production of OH• radicals; (ii) OH• radicals were detected through the formation of fluorescent 2-hydroxy terephthalate on reaction with disodium terephthalate. B) Digital photographs showing the generation of ROS at various pH 7.4, 6.4 and 5.5 without or within the presence of H2O2 and fluorescence emission spectra of LCN@Mn-BA at different pH 7.4, 6.4 and 5.5 in the presence of H2O2. C) Images showing fluorescence emissions of MNP nanoparticles dispersion at different excitation wavelengths and D) corresponding fluorescence emission spectra. E) In vitro drug release profile of LCN@Mn-BA at various pH conditions (pH 7.4, 6.4, 5.5 at 37 °C) for 48 h. F) Physical stability studies of LCN@Mn-BA conducted in PBS pH 7.4 at 25 °C for six months. All the (E,F) data are expressed in mean ± S.D, n = 3.

A) Cytotoxic activity of BA and Mn in MDA-MB-231 cell line over a period of 48 h treatment. B) Predicted inhibition data of Bliss synergy model in MDA-MB-231 cells treated with BA on X-axis and Mn on Y-axis in 5 × 6 concentration checker board format for 48 h using the MTT assay; In the Combenefit matrix view, the synergy spots are represented in blue color. C) Comparative growth inhibition data; and D) combination index data of different LCN@Mn-BA formulations loaded with various synergistic combinations of free drugs (Mn and BA) and free Mn+BA cocktail obtained from combination experiment in MDA-MB-231 cells. All the data are expressed as mean ± SD, n = 3. Statistical significance: *** p ≤ 0.001.

A) Confocal images representing the cellular uptake of LCN@Mn-BA nanoparticles at different time points in MDA-MB-231 cell lines; B) Overlay of fluorescence intensity data obtained by flow cytometer; C) Mean fluorescent intensity (MFI) of MDA-MB-231 cell lines incubated with LCN@Mn-BA nanoparticles at different time points; All the MFI data are shown as mean ± SD (n = 3).

A,B) Representative cell cycle arrest data performed in MDA-MB-231 cell lines for 24 h after incubation with free drugs (Mn, BA), (Mn+BA) drug cocktail, LCN@Mn, LCN@Mn-BA formulations equivalent to BA (0.3 μg/mL) and Mn (1.25 μg/mL) and plain media as a control. C,D) Representative apoptosis in MDA-MB-231 cells after treatment with above mentioned formulations equivalent to Mn (5 μg/mL) and/or BA (1.25 μg/mL), and plain media as a control for 24 h; the data are shown as mean ± SD (n = 3).

A) ROS analysis- amplification of oxidative stress in MDA-MB-231 cell lines by free drugs and (Mn+BA) cocktail, drug loaded nanoparticles (LCN@Mn, LCN@Mn-BA) equivalent Mn (5 μg/mL) and/or BA (1.25 μg/mL), for 24 h. PBS treated cells were used as a negative control and 10 μM H2O2 treated cells served as a positive control. Oxidative stress in MDA-MB-231 cell line are represented in density plot and B) Mean fluorescence intensity of MDA-MB-231 cell line after treatment for 24 h. C) Fluorescence dye test for the determination of OH• free radical in MDA-MB-231 cell lines by free drugs and (Mn+BA) cocktail, drug loaded nanoparticles (LCN@Mn, LCN@Mn-BA) equivalent Mn (5 μg/mL) and/or BA (1.25 μg/mL), for 12 h. All the data are shown as mean ± SD (n = 3).

In vivo tumor imaging after i.v. injection of LCN@Mn-BA formulation (1 mg/mL MNP equivalent); A) Body imaging of control and treated group; B) Ex-vivo imaging of control and LCN@Mn-BA treated group organs at 24 h of post injection; C, D) In vivo quantification of tumor and organs associated fluorescence signals of LCN@Mn-BA nanoparticles.

In vivo Pharmacokinetics and tumor distribution studies. A) BA and B) Mn concentration in plasma of free drug cocktail and LCN@Mn-BA formulation at a dose of 10 mg/kg of BA and 40 mg kg of Mn were used in the study. C) BA and D) Mn concentration in tumor at different time points; E) Comparative data showing the presence ratio of BA and Mn present at tumor site. All the data are shown as mean ± SD (n = 3); *p < 0.05, **p < 0.01, ***p < 0.001 (n = 5 in each treatment group).

In vivo therapeutic performance of LCN@Mn-BA against 4T1 tumor xenografts. A) tumor volume; B) tumor weight; and C) body weight of 4T1 tumor bearing mice treated with saline (control group), Mn, BA, Mn+BA cocktail, LCN@Mn and LCN@Mn-BA. D) Representative image of tumors dissected at the end of 21 days study. E) The percentage of survival of 4T1 tumor-bearing mice treated with Mn, BA, Mn+BA cocktail, LCN@Mn, LCN@Mn-BA and saline (control group). The tumor volume, tumor weight, average body weight and survival rate of the all mice are described as the mean ± SD. The free drugs or LCN's formulation equivalent to dose of 10 mg/kg of BA and 40 mg/kg of Mn were used in the study.*p < 0.05, **p < 0.01, ***p < 0.001 (n = 5 in each treatment group).

In vivo tissue toxicity assessment of LCN@Mn-BA nanoparticles. Representative H&E stained images of major organs of mice (tumor, liver, kidney, lung, spleen, and heart) harvested from the control (PBS solution), Mn+BA cocktail solution, LCN@Mn-BA nanoparticles treated groups. Scale 20X magnification.

A) i) Schematic illustration of synthesis of Mn-Oleate (MO) complex and a fluorescent & Fenton type catalyst i.e. MnO nanoparticles (MNPs), ii) LCN's were prepared using phytantriol (PHYT) and MO complex and then loaded with MNPs, B) Schematic depiction of the mechanism of action of LCN@Mn-BA nanoparticles that release simultaneously betulinic acid (anti-cancer drug) and fluorescent MNPs which selectively amplify oxidative stress in cancer cells without any apparent toxicity in normal cells.

ce:legend.ce:simple-para: All values are represented as mean ± SD (n=3).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Pharmacokinetic parameters of BA and Mn in mice plasma after single intravenous administration of BA+Mn cocktail and LCN@Mn-BA formulations at a dose of 10 mg/kg BA and 40 mg/kg Mn.

ce:legend.ce:simple-para: Statistical significance: ***p ≤ 0.001; **p ≤ 0.01; *p ≤ 0.05, and all the data are represented as mean ± S.D (n=3).

head.ce:title: Theranostic lyotropic liquid crystalline nanostructures for selective breast cancer imaging and therapy

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Herein, we report the development of theranostic lyotropic liquid crystalline nanostructures (LCN's) loaded with unique MnO nanoparticles (MNPs) for selective cancer imaging and therapy. MNPs serves as a fluorescent agent as well as a source of manganese (Mn2+) and enables localized oxidative stress under the hallmarks of cancer (acidosis, high H2O2 level). In pursuit of synergistic amplification of Mn2+ antitumor activity, betulinic acid (BA) is loaded in LCN's. In this investigation, nano-architecture of LCN's phase interface is established via SAXS, Cryo-TEM and Cryo-FESEM. Intriguing in vitro studies showed that the LCN's triggered hydroxyl radical production and exhibited greater selective cytotoxicity in cancer cells, ensuring the safety of normal cells. Significant tumor ablation is realized by the 96.5 % of tumor growth inhibition index of LCN's as compared to control group. Key insights into on-site drug release, local anti-cancer response, and tumor location are gained through precise guidance of fluorescent MNPs. In addition, comprehensive assessment of the safety, pharmacokinetics and tumor distribution behavior of LCN's is performed in vivo or ex vivo. This work emphasizes the promise of modulating tumor microenvironment with smart endogenous stimuli sensitive nano systems to achieve advanced comprehensive cancer nano-theranostics without any external stimulus.

Effective diagnosis and treatment approaches with maximum anti-cancer activity and minimal side-effects are critical to ameliorate cancer therapy. Compared to radiation, photodynamic and photothermal therapy, the specific and selective activation of tumor microenvironmental endogenous stimuli for the logical generation of cytotoxic OH· free radicals serves as an efficient therapeutic strategy for chemodynamic-cancer treatment. In this investigation, MnO nanoparticles fulfill two needs (fluorescence-based optical imaging and a source of Mn2+ based chemodynamic therapy) in one unit. This approach also ensures the safety of normal cells, as the toxic OH· free radical activity is substantially suppressed under the mild alkaline/H2O2 conditions in normal cell microenvironment.

ce:section.ce:para: Cancer theranostics is a seamless combination of therapeutics and diagnostics into a single platform aimed at propelling oncology towards an era of more effective and personalized therapy [1]. But, the severe side effects of conventional chemotherapy in cancer treatment and its deficient tumor specificity open up a debate on the clinical significance of simply integrating chemotherapeutics and diagnostic agents into a new 'theranostics' [2]. Until recently, the emergence of alternative therapeutic modalities such as optoacoustic therapy, ultrasound therapy, photodynamic therapy and other thermo-ablative strategies brings up new opportunities and space for theranostics [3]. However, their incomplete tumor ablation, low signal penetration into deep cancer sites, recurrence of disease, poor signal-to-noise ratio, false positive outputs are causing disappointing results. Consequently, the design of intelligent theranostics with versatile properties capable of simultaneous monitoring and specific cancer therapy remains a major challenge.

Cancer is generally considered as an anomalous tissue with a special microenvironment and complex microstructure. Unlike normal cells, the tumor microenvironment (TME) usually has mild acidic conditions (pH 6.4-6.8) and increased levels of H2O2 due to inadequate blood supply and rapid metabolism of malignant cells [4,5]. Acidosis and high H2O2 levels are the universal hallmark of solid tumors regardless of their tumor type or stage of development. Therefore, these unique physicochemical conditions and biosynthetic intermediates of TME's can be used to specifically kill cancer cells. In the Fenton or Fenton-like reaction, transition metal ions enable the disproportionation of H2O2 to efficiently generate harmful reactive oxygen species (ROS) such as hydroxyl (OH•) radicals thereby selectively increases oxidative stress in cancer cells [6–9]. This leverages for greater therapeutic effectiveness and accurate diagnosis with minimal damage to healthy cells. Metal ion such as Fe2+ ion triggered reaction is however only effective under highly acidic environment (pH 2–4) and is relatively ineffective under weakly acidic TME [10,11]. Consequently, more specific Fenton reactors active in mild acidic TME with high selectivity and catalytic activity are highly desirable. Manganese (Mn) has been a valuable diagnostic agent and therapeutic tool for studying and treating various disease conditions since its inception in 1979 [12]. To date, simple Mn2+ salts and Mn2+ containing thio-semicarbazone or hydrazone-complex groups have been reported to have a good anti-proliferative effect on various cancer cell lines [13]. Recent evidences have shown that Mn2+ like Fenton type catalyst can trigger ROS-mediated cell death with high efficiency even in weak acidic conditions (pH 6.4–6.8) and Fenton-like reaction catalyzed by Mn2+ is kinetically and energetically better than Fenton reaction catalyzed by Fe2+ [14–17].

In this context, we report a strategy to fabricate Mn-functionalized lyotropic liquid crystalline nanoparticles (LCN) as a new theranostic arsenal for tumor specific imaging and therapy. LCNs are non-lamellar liquid crystalline phase particles, that have been extensively demonstrated to be biocompatible both in vitro and in vivo [18]. The inherent drawbacks allied with conventional liposomes made LCNs as an alternative cargo system for biomedical applications [19–24].

In this study as a dual modal fluorescent & Fenton-type catalyst, we used Mn-oxide (MnO) nanoparticles and loaded them into LCN (LCN@Mn). By simply carbonizing the oleate of the Mn-Oleate complex (MO) during the thermal decomposition process, MnO nanoparticles (MNPs) were formed together with fluorescent component, no additional conjugation or encapsulation of the secondary fluorescent nanoprobe is required [25–27]. These MNPs serve as an efficient fluorescent nanoprobe for multicolor in vitro and in vivo imaging in entire visible spectrum range (400–700 nm). In addition to that, under mild acidic TME conditions MNPs forms Mn2+ ions and catalyzes Fenton-like reaction to generate OH• radical that triggers apoptosis and inhibits the progression of tumor (Chemodynamic therapy) [28–32].

Current anticancer treatment such as photodynamic therapy relies on a number of external stimulating factors such as light irradiation with an appropriate wavelength, a photosensitizer, and oxygen to produce reactive oxygen species. However, unlike photodynamic therapy, chemodynamic therapy does not require external energy stimuli, it works on the basis of Fenton-like reaction and internal stimuli such as cancer's mild acidic condition, high H2O2 levels to induce intracellular oxidative stress in cancer cells. Chemodynamic therapy would therefore improve on or solve the existing problems such as oxygen dependence, inefficient photosensitizer and restricted light penetration.

In addition to that it has the following advantages: 1) chemodynamic therapy is very selective, logical and has a specific treatment strategy as compared to chemotherapy, photothermal and radiotherapy which shows non-negligible side effects [33]. 2) It also works under the hypoxic environment of cancer cells [34]. 3) It activates by cancer cell specific endogenous stimulus [35] and 4) this approach ensures greater safety towards normal cells as Fenton reaction is substantially suppressed in the normal microenvironment of cells under slight alkaline conditions and with a lower amount of H2O2 [36–40].

Conveyance of active chemotherapeutics in synergy with these Fenton nanoreactors to malignant cells is a reasonable approach to amplify Mn anticancer activity. Along these lines, liquid crystalline nanoparticles (LCN@Mn-BA) was set up by loading betulinic acid (BA), a triterpenoid with anticancer activity. BA has an interesting antiproliferative effect against a panel of cancers including epithelial cancers and malignant melanomas, and it rarely affects normal cells raised its safety and therapeutic window [41]. In addition, different studies on various cell lines were compared in which normal cell lines showed that they were resistant to BA than cancer cells, which reflected its tumor selectivity [42]. It is known that BA, independent of the P 53 status, causes direct cell death and also circumvents normal resistance to chemotherapy in cancer cells [43,44]. These properties of BA make it an interesting candidate for the drug selection.

In this investigation, we report the lyotropic liquid crystalline nanostructures, which combines the anticancer activity and diagnostic capabilities with the goal of developing tumor targeting theranostics while safeguarding the normal cells.
The efficiency of our LCN@Mn-BA was demonstrated by various physicochemical characterization techniques, in vitro and in vivo experiments.

Manganese chloride tetrahydrate (MnCl2.4H2O), sodium salt of oleic acid (OA), phytantriol (PHYT), pluronic F 127 (PF127), betulinic acid (BA) diethylether, methylthiazolyldiphenyl-tetrazolium bromide (MTT), disodium terephthalate (TA), dichlorofluorescein diacetate (DCFDA), 2-hydroxyterephthalic disodium (HTA) were purchased from TCI chemicals, India. Annexin V-FITC based apoptosis kit and propidium iodide (PI), HPLC grade solvents were purchased from Merck, India. Hexane, octadecene, ethanol, and other chemicals and solvents of analytical grade were purchased from other commercial sources.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of Mn-Oleate complex, and MnO nanoparticles
ce:section.ce:para: The manganese-oleate complex (MO) was obtained by reaction of MnCl2.4H2O (5 mmol) with excessive sodium salt of oleic acid (OA) (15 mmol). In short, 0.99 g of MnCl2.4H2O and 4.65 g of OA was dissolved in a solvent blend consisting of distilled water (7.5 mL), ethanol (10 mL), and hexane (17.5 mL). The resulting solution was kept for 4 h at 70 °C. After completion of the reaction, the resultant supernatant containing the MO complex, was collected and washed thrice using distilled water and then vacuum dried.

In a representative synthesis of < 10 nm manganese-oxide (MnO) nanoparticles, 1 g of MO dissolved in 10 mL of octadecene was heated in a step-by-step manner starting from 100 °C for 30 min under vacuum and sequentially raised the temperature to 200 °C for 20 min and 280 °C for 10 min under inert atmosphere. When the temperature of reaction reached 280 °C, the initial transparent red solution became brownish black. The resulting solution was cooled to room temperature (RT), centrifuged (7084 g, 15 min) and MnO nanoparticles (MNPs) were collected and washed thrice with ethanol. The purified nanoparticles were re-dispersed in hexane for further use.

The method employed in the preparation of manganese (Mn) based liquid crystalline nanoparticles was slightly modified method reported by L Rodrigues et al [45]. It involves dispersion of an appropriate amount of phytantriol (PHYT), MO complex, and phosphate buffer saline (PBS) buffer pH 7.4 solution of pluronic F 127 (PF127). Dispersion was stored in dark for three days at RT to achieve equilibrium, followed by sonication (Vibra-Cell VCX 750, Sonics & Materials. Inc. CT, USA) at 40 amplitude power for 10 min in pulse mode (1s off and 1s on). In the similar fashion, doped LCN's were prepared by dispersing the betulinic acid (BA, 2.8 × 10−1 wt%), and MNPs (11 × 10−1 wt%) in the melted PHYT (96 mg/mL) before equilibrium. In a ratio of 94:6 w/w %, PHYT and MO were mixed. The ultimate PF127 concentration in solution was 1 wt%.

The MO complex and MNPs were characterized by means of Fourier transformed infrared spectroscopy (FTIR), proton nuclear magnetic resonance spectroscopy (1H NMR), high resolution X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (HR-XPS), selected area energy diffraction (SAED), energy dispersive X-ray spectrometer (EDS), atomic absorption spectroscopy (AAS), quasi-elastic light scattering experiment (QELS), small-angle X-ray scattering (SAXS), high resolution transmission electron microscopy (HR-TEM), cryogenic transmission electron microscopy (CryoTEM), cryogenic force emission scanning electron microscopy (Cryo-FESEM), UV–vis spectrophotometry, fluorescence spectroscopy.

All the FTIR spectrums were analyzed on a PerkinElmer Spectrum II. All the All H1-NMR spectras were recorded using CDCl3 as a solvent on a 300 MHz Bruker DRX-300 (Bruker, Switzerland). Particle size and particle size distributions of nanoparticles were analyzed on a Nano ZS (Malvern Instruments, U.K.) equipped with He-Ne laser (633 nm), and 173° collecting optics. SAXS studies were carried on HECUS X-ray system, Graz, Austria) quipped with S3-Micro SAXS/SWAXS camera, HECUS PSD50M detector containing 1024 channels of width 54.0 μm, and GeniX X-ray generator for Cu Kα radiation. The working q range was 0.0–0.6 Å−1. HR-TEM and EDS images were recorded on a FEI Titan G2 60-300 with a 300 kV field emission gun. CryoTEM and SAED images were acquired on a FEI Tecnai G2 12 Twin and HOLEY C 300M AU grid was used. Cryo-FESEM images are captured by using ZEISS ULTRA plus Cryo-FESEM (Carl Zeiss International, Germany). AFM images was taken on APE research AFM (APCER, Italy).AAS analysis was carried out on Perkin-Elmer Pinaacle 900T. For AAS measurements samples were washed with ethanol to precipitate the inorganic material and then added nitric acid to oxidize all the organic matter. Inductively coupled plasma mass spectrometry data was acquired on (ICP-MS, 2030, shimadzu, Japan), HR-XPS data was acquired from PHI 5000 Versa Probe II, FEI Inc. Photophysical characterization of nanoparticles were carried out on UV–vis spectrometer (Shimadzu Corporation, Tokyo, Japan). Fluorescence measurements were recorded on (RF 6000 spectro fluorophotometer, Shimadzu corporation, Japan). The fluorescence quantum yield of MNP particles were measured using Indocyanine green dissolved in methanol as a reference standard (Q.Yref=0.078).

ce:section.ce:section-title: pH-dependent LCN's behavior and drug release
ce:section.ce:para: The release of manganese (Mn) and BA was assessed using dialysis bag method (dialysis bag-MWCO 1000 D) at 37 °C under different pH conditions (pH 7.4, 6.4, 5.5) [46]. In short LCN@Mn-BA containing the equivalent concentration of Mn (3 mg) and BA (0.75 mg) were dialyzed against 50 mL of PBS (pH 7.4, 6.4, 5.5) containing 0.1% Tween-80 and rotated continuously at 100 rpm. At appropriate time intervals, aliquots of media were removed and replaced by fresh media. The final concentration of BA was measured after dialysis using the UV–vis spectrometer and the concentration of Mn was measured using the AAS.

The long-term physical stability of the LCN@Mn-BA was determined by incubating the LCN@Mn-BA nanoparticles in PBS pH 7.4 at 25 °C for 6 months. In regular intervals of time samples were collected for the examination of size and polydispersity index (PDI) of the nanoparticles using quasi-elastic light scattering experiment (QELS) method. In addition, to know its in vivo fate, nanoparticles were also incubated in 10% FBS for 24 h, and changes in size and PDI were recorded. All these studies were conducted in triplicate.

The method used for the hemolysis assay was as follows. Briefly, fresh blood from mice was collected and centrifuged to separate red blood cells (RBC). 2% hemocrit was prepared with PBS and to this blank formulation along with free drugs (BA, Mn) and formulations (LCN@Mn, LCN@Mn-BA) containing BA and Mn were added to incubate for 2 h at 37 °C at different concentrations. Finally, haemoglobin content was determined using microplate reader at a wavelength of 540 nm. 1% Triton X-100 in PBS was used as positive control and plain PBS was used as a negative control.

ce:section.ce:section-title: In vitro catalytic process by Mn2+ and Fe+2
ce:section.ce:para: Using the exemplary fluorescent strategy dependent on disodium terephthalate (TA), a selective OH• radical scavenger, the OH• radical generation by the LCN@Mn Fenton nanoreactors was evaluated. Disodium TA reacts with OH• radical and generates fluorescent 2-hydroxy terephthalic disodium (HTA). The fluorescence intensity of 10 μg/mL TA solution (in 25 mM NaHCO3/ 5% CO2 buffer at 7.4, 6.4, 5.5 pH), with or without 8 mM H2O2 was measured using spectrofluorometer under excitation of 320 nm and emission at 425 nm before and after addition of 1 mM equivalent Mn containing LCN@Mn nanoparticles.
Similarly, Fenton-like catalytic activity of 1 mM of Mn2+ and Fe2+ was also compared by using HTA assay under different pH (pH 1-8) conditions [47].

4T1 and MDA-MB-231 breast cancer cell lines were obtained from the American Type Culture Collection (Rockville, MD). Non-malignant origin cell line, HEK-293 (human embryonic kidney) was obtained from institutional cell repository of animal tissue culture facility. Both the cells were maintained in Roswell Park Memorial Institute meddium-1640 (RPMI-1640) with 10% v/v FBS, along with 100 I.U. penicillin, and 100 μg/mL streptomycin. All cells were incubated at 37 °C in a 90% relative humidified environment with 5% CO2.

4T1 and MDA-MB-231 cells were used for in vitro cytotoxicity evaluation of free Mn, free BA, Mn+BA cocktail, LCN@Mn, LCN@Mn-BA, via methylthiazolyldiphenyl-tetrazolium bromide (MTT) assay. Cytotoxicity studies were initially performed for plain drugs Mn-(1–80 μg/mL) and BA-(0.5–20 μg/mL) for 48 h to identify the synergy combination. After finding the synergistic ratio, Mn+BA cocktail and LCN@Mn-BA comparative cytotoxicity studies were conducted with an equivalent concentration of BA and Mn in a four different combinations; BA/Mn-(1.25/5 μg/mL), (1.25/10 μg/mL), (0.5/5 μg/mL) and (0.5/10 μg/mL). In brief, cells (3000–3500 per well) were seeded in 96-well plates and allowed overnight to get adhered. After that cells were treated with Mn, BA, Mn+BA, LCN@Mn, LCN@Mn-BA in RPMI media with 2%FBS. After incubation for predetermined time, media was removed and 20 μL of MTT reagent (5 mg/mL) was added and 100 μL of the mixture of DMSO/IPA (1:1) was added to the formazan crystals of live cells after 4 h. On a microplate reader at 570 nm absorption was taken, while the viability of treated cells was normalized to untreated cells on the same plate [48,49].
In addition, the toxicity of LCN@Mn-BA was determined on the HEK 293 cell line to evaluate the biocompatibility of developed liquid crystalline particles.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Analysis of synergy from combination studies
ce:section.ce:para: The data from the MTT assay was analyzed using the Combenefit software to determine the possible synergistic or additive effect when using the different combination of Mn and BA. The software independently calculates the synergy / additive score for each combination with three published models (Highest Single Agent (HAS), Bliss, and Loewe). A positive (+ ve) score indicates synergy and negative (-ve) score indicates antagonism and 0 is an additive. We used Bliss model in this combination experiment because it assumes independent mechanisms of action of two drugs. In addition, results were analysed for the combination index of the drug combination using the CompuSyn software according to the Chou-Talalay model. Where a combination index (CI) value < 1 indicates synergy, CI value > 1 and CI= 1 indicate antagonism and additive effect respectively [50].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Cellular uptake and cell imaging studies

ce:section.ce:para: The MDA-MB-231 cells and 4T1 cells were grown overnight in RPMI media supplemented with 10% FBS. After the well growth of the cells, an aliquot of the LCN@Mn-BA suspension was added to the cultured plate, then incubated for 12 h at 37 °C in a 5% CO2 incubator. Before fixing the cells on the slide for confocal microscopy inspection, the excess LCN@Mn-BA was removed by washing the cells with PBS pH 7.4. Bioimaging was taken in a confocal microscope (Carl Zeiss LSM510 META, Carl Zeiss, Germany).

A quantitative evaluation was carried out using a flow cytometer (BD Biosciences, FACS Aria, Germany), for the uptake of the nanoparticles in MBA-MB 231 and 4T1 cells. In short, the cells were plated in a density of 3 × 105 cells/well and incubated in a 5% CO2 incubator for overnight at 37 °C. The cells were treated with a LCN@Mn-BA formulation aliquot equivalent to BA (0.3 μg/ml) and Mn (1.25 μg/mL) for 12 h after incubation. Following that cell-associated fluorescence was measured using 405 nm laser with a band pass filter of 450/20 for the respective time intervals.

MDA-MB-231 cells grown in a 6 well plate with a density of 3 × 105 cell/ well and then, after overnight incubation, treated with free drugs and different formulation at the concentration as mentioned above for 24 h. Cells were washed with fresh PBS, trypsinized, re-suspended in 500 μL cold 70% ethanol, and allowed for 2 h at 4 °C. Following ethanol fixation, cells were centrifuged and then in 350 μL of fresh PBS resuspended. Then treated cells with 50 μL (100 μg/mL) ribonuclease A (Rnase A), 100 μL (100 μg/mL) propidiuim iodide (PI) and allowed for 30 min. Then cell DNA content was analyzed using flow cytometer (BD Biosciences, FACS Calibur, Germany).

MDA-MB-231 cells cultured in a 6 well plate were treated with free drugs and different formulation equivalent to Mn (5 μg/mL) and/or BA (1.25 μg/mL) for 24 h. Followed by washing with fresh PBS the cells were re-suspended in 1X binding buffer and then stained with fluorogens Annexin V-FITC and PI. After that by gentle mixing and incubated for 20 min in dark room, the stained cells were analyzed using flow cytometer (BD Biosciences, FACS Calibur, Germany).

To determine the MMP of the treated cancer cells, JC-1 dye was used. In brief, pre-seeded cells (5 × 104 cells/ well) were treated with free drugs (BA, Mn) and formulation (LCN@Mn, LCN@Mn-BA) at an equivalent concentration of Mn (5 μg/mL) and/or BA (1.25 μg/mL) for 24 h at 37 °C. Subsequently, after treatment cells were washed and 10 μg/mL of JC-1 dye was added and incubated for 30 min. Then cells fluorescence was measured using microplate reader.

For ROS detection, cells were treated with dichloro-dihydro-fluorescein diacetate (DCFH-DA) dye. Initially, MDA-MB-231 cells were seeded (1 × 105 cells/well) in 6 well plate and incubated overnight at 37 °C and 5% CO2. After this pre-warmed 10 μM DCFH-DA dye was added and incubated at RT for 60 min. Following this, the dye working solution was removed and the cells were washed with PBS before being treated with free drugs and different formulations containing an equivalent concentration of Mn (5 μg/mL) and/or BA (1.25 μg/mL) for 24 h. Finally, the resulting ROS was measured using the flow cytometer (BD Biosciences, FACS CALIBUR, Germany) by fluorescence intensity of 2,7-dichlorofluorescence (DCF). As a positive control 10 μM H2O2 treated cells were used and untreated cells were used as a negative control. All experiments were carried out thrice (n = 3).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Determination of OH• free radical using hydroxyphenyl fluorescein (HPF)
ce:section.ce:para: In addition to the above test for determination of OH• free radical, OH• selective fluorescent dye hydroxyphenyl fluorescein (HPF) was used. In brief, 10 µM of HPF was added to pre-seeded MDA-MB-231 cells and incubated for 20 min, followed by the treatment with the various formulation mentioned as above for 12 h. Finally, the HPF fluorescence was determined at an excitation and emission of 488 nm and 515 nm respectively. All experiments were carried out thrice (n = 3).

Female BALB/c mice were obtained from CDRI's National Laboratory Animal Center (NLAC).To evaluate the theranostic property of LCN@Mn-BA in vivo, a mouse model bearing a 4T1 cell-derived xenograft was established by subcutaneous injection of 5 × 106 number of 4T1 cells near mammary glands of a mouse [51]. After inoculation, regular observation of animals was made, and after ∼12 days when the tumor grew to about 100 mm3, studies were started and carried out up to 21 days post tumor formation.
The institutional animal ethical committee has approved all animal studies (IAEC approval No.-IAEC/2018/F-28).

ce:section.ce:section-title: In vivo and ex vivo imaging studies
ce:section.ce:para: When the tumor volume reached 100 mm3, 4T1 tumor-bearing mice were injected with PBS and LCN@Mn-BA (dose, 1 mg/mL MNP equivalent) via tail vein. In vivo imaging was performed on a Caliper IVIS Kinetic In Vivo Optical Imaging System (Caliper Life Sciences) at 1, 4, 8, 12 and 24 h post injection with 610 nm excitation and 660 nm emission. All the mice were sacrificed at 24 h and the organs were collected for ex vivo imaging. Finally, images were analyzed using the in vivo imaging software (Living image software version 4.4). All experiments were conducted in triplicate.

ce:section.ce:section-title: In vivo pharmacokinetics and tumor distribution studies
ce:section.ce:para: In order to explore the in vivo pharmacokinetics and distribution of free drugs (BA+Mn) cocktail and LCN@Mn-BA formulation in tumor, breast cancer induced mice model was used and both the formulations were injected (i.v) at a dose of 10 mg/kg BA and 40 mg/kg Mn. Two mice groups were used; group-I for free BA+Mn cocktail and group-II for LCN@Mn-BA formulation and at predetermined time points 200 μL of blood was collected from retro-orbital plexus. In a similar fashion after predetermined time tumors were harvested and analyzed for the both the drugs. Validated HPLC method was used for the determination of BA and ICP-MS technique was used for the determination of Mn. Prior to Mn analysis, the blood samples were digested with aqua regia followed by dilution with milli-Q water before ICP-MS analysis. For RP-HPLC analysis, mobile phase consist of acetontrile (A) and milli-Q water pH adjusted to 3.0 with phosphoric acid (B) was used. Flow was isocratic at the ratio of 70:30 (A: B). Later, plasma concentration– time profiles were determined using non-compartmental analysis with the aid of PKPlusTM software.

ce:section.ce:section-title: In vivo antitumor study in a 4T1-tumor bearing BALB/c mice
ce:section.ce:para: As per the above-mentioned protocol, female BALB/c mice bearing 4T1 cells derived-xenograft was prepared. When the volume of the tumor reached 100 mm3, mice were randomly divided into six groups (n = 5 in each group). The mice in the respective groups were injected intravenously with 100 µL of PBS, free Mn, BA, Mn+BA cocktail, LCN@Mn, LCN@Mn-BA nanoparticles dispersion (at an equivalent dose of 10 mg/kg BA and 40 mg/kg Mn) for individual mice on day 1, 4, 8, 12, 16 and sacrificed on 21st day of treatment started. Growth of the tumor and body weight were monitored throughout the study. The tumor volume (TV) was evaluated by measuring the tumor length (L) and width (W) and calculated as TV= (L x W2)/2. On day 21, post-treatment mice were sacrificed and tumor tissues were collected and weighed to determine the tumor growth inhibition index (TIX) and calculated as a % TIX=[1-(T / C)] x 100. Where, T= mean volume of treated tumor volume and C= mean volume of control tumors.

At the end of the tumour regression study, mice were sacrificed. Then the mice main organs such as heart, liver, spleen, lung, kidney, and tumor were harvested and stored for overnight in 10% of the paraformaldehyde solution. Samples of the tissue were then fixed in paraffin wax, sliced (5 μm) and stained with hematoxyllin and eosin (H&E) dyes. All the collected tissues samples were imaged using an optical microscope under 20X magnification [52,53].

For the survival analysis study mice were divided into six different groups (n = 5), and they were induced with tumor and allowed it to reach 100 mm3 volume. Treatment started as per the protocol mentioned above. The survival rate of animals in these five different groups was measured for 50 days and analyzed using Graphpad Prism version 5.0 Software. Using the log-rank test method survival curves were evaluated.[54]

Statistical values are expressed as mean ± s.d. For the comparison of different groups by student t-test or by one-way ANOVA GraphPad Prism version 5.0 (San Diego, CA) was used. The p < 0.001 difference was considered statistically significant.
Scheme 1 portraits the complete picture of the current investigation along with the working principle.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation and characterization of Mn-Oleate complex and MnO nanoparticles
ce:section.ce:para: In short as shown in the Fig. 1 A, manganese (Mn) chloride and oleic acid (OA) sodium salt were dissolved in solvent mixture (ethanol/distilled water/hexane), then the resulting mixture was heated at 70 °C for 4 h to get the Mn-oleate (MO) complex. Further by using thermal decomposition process, MO was heated in a step-by-step manner starting from 100 °C for 30 min under vacuum and sequentially raised the temperature to 200 °C for 20 min and 280 °C for 10 min under inert atmosphere to form carbonaceous-fluorescent component in line with the formation of Mn-oxide (MnO) nanoparticles in a high boiling point solvent (octadecene). As shown in the (Fig. 1B) during this process MO complex's initial transparent red color changed to dark brown, which marks the formation of MnO nanoparticles (MNPs).

Then a comparative study of Fourier transformed infrared spectroscopy (FTIR) was performed between OA, MO and MNPs from 4000 to 400 cm−1 (Fig. 1C). The characteristic oleate bands were shown in all the spectra. Strong vibrational regions from 3012 to 2872 cm−1 and from 1689 to 1453 cm−1 can be assigned to-CH2 stretching and carboxyl -COO stretching respectively in the OA spectrum. However, in comparison, the intensity of these bands was reduced in the MNP sample. It may be due to the carbonization of oleate in the MO during the thermal decomposition process, resulting in the simultaneous formation of MNPs along with carbonaceous components. Characterization through proton nuclear magnetic resonance spectroscopy also confirmed the formation of both MO and MNPs (Fig. 1D). The vinyl and allyl proton resonance of oleic acid was appeared at 5.4 and 2.1 ppm in OA and MO spectrum but not in the MNP spectrum. Moreover, large broadening effect was observed in the spectrum of MNP, due to large magnetic moment effect of paramagnetic materials present in the MNPs.

Fig. S1 shows the high resolution transmission electron microscopy images of MNPs with an average diameter of 7.3 ± 2 nm calculated using ImageJ software (from 380 individual particles) (particle size distribution data- Fig. S2). High resolution images show the lattice fringes of MNPs with a diameter of 0.157 nm corresponding to the 220 plane of manganese monoxide particles and this was further confirmed by the diffraction rings in selected area energy diffraction patterns.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of LCN nanoparticles and its characterization
ce:section.ce:para: Non-drug loaded (LCN@Mn) and drug loaded (LCN@Mn-BA) nanoparticles were prepared as follows. Initially, LCN@Mn were prepared using phytantriol (PHYT), MO and pluronic F 127 (PF127) and then MNPs (11 × 10−1 wt%) were loaded further into it. Then to boost Mn's anti-cancer activity, LCN@Mn-BA was set up by loading BA (2.8 × 10−1 wt%) dependent on the synergistic proportion of Mn and BA obtained from the drug combination experiment. Subsequent to loading of MNP and BA, the cargo unequivocally intercalated inside the lipid center of the LCN's due to their hydrophobic nature, and entrapment efficiency of fluorescent MNP and BA in the formulation was found to be 95 ± 2% and 96 ± 3% respectively.

Fig. 2 A and B show the cryogenic field emission scanning electron microscopy (Cryo-FESEM) images of the liquid crystalline particles (LCN@Mn and LCN@Mn-BA) taken by freeze fracture & freeze etching technique. Fig. 2C and D in the insets show the images of LCN@Mn and LCN@Mn-BA obtained through cryogenic transmission electron microscopy (CryoTEM). Cryo-FESEM revealed the clear three dimensional hexagonal like structure of the vitrified liquid crystalline nanoparticles in agreement with CryoTEM images. Moreover the size of the nanoparticles got through quasi-elastic light scattering experiment (QELS) were in great concurrence with the dimensions obtained with Cryo-FESEM and CryoTEM technique (Fig. 2E and F). Moreover, the size of the nanoparticles obtained was appropriate for collection at the site of tumor by enhanced permeation retention (EPR) effect. The size and zeta potential values of the LCN@Mn, LCN@Mn-BA obtained through QELS analysis are given in Table 1 and Fig. S3.

Further we have investigated the stage at which LCN@Mn and LCN@Mn-BA nanoparticles exist through small-angle X-ray scattering (SAXS) analysis. The information from the SAXS (Fig. 2I and J) illustrates that these nanoparticles showed three Bragg peaks with relative position at spacing ratio of 1:√3:2 consistent with the inverse hexagonal phase (HII). Structural parameters such as lattice parameters and water channel radii of LCN's are listed in Table 1. After all, atomic force microscopy (AFM) (Fig. S4) was of interest as complementary method for the analysis of the size of the final formulation (LCN@Mn-BA). Moreover, the average size of the LCN@Mn-BA nanoparticles obtained from AFM and other different microscopic techniques was AFM (170-200 nm), Cryo-FESEM (180-200 nm), CryoTEM (160-180 nm).

To confirm the loading of fluorescent MNPs nanoparticles in the final formulation (LCN@Mn-BA), we have taken the fluorescent images of LCN@Mn-BA and a blank formulation without MNPs. As shown in the Fig. 2G and H the MNPs loaded liquid crystalline particles emitted bright red fluorescence at a wavelength of 633 nm in contrast to the blank formulation. In addition, Transmission electron microscopy analysis was also performed, successful incorporation of MNPs were proved (Fig. S5).

Then the Energy dispersive X-ray spectrometer (EDS) technique was used for the final formulation elemental analysis. Presence of Mn, O elements was confirmed in the formulation by its unique set of peaks in the electromagnetic emission spectrum (Fig. 2K). Advance characterization of LCN@Mn-BA through high resolution X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (HR-XPS), showed Mn2p1/2 Mn2p3/2 signals located at 651.8 eV, 640.32 eV and a satellite peak at 645.9 eV (Fig. 2L). These values are in good concurrence with the literature values of Mn2+ in bulk MnO particles [55]. These results confirmed, the loading of MNPs in the LCN@Mn-BA and also asserted that Mn2+ is not oxidized to Mn3+ during formulation process.

Disodium terephthalate (TA) based fluorescence method was used to determine the Fenton-like catalyst activity of LCN@Mn-BA in the presence of H2O2, and we explored it by generating OH• radicals under different pH conditions. TA reacts specially with OH• radical and generates fluorescent 2-hydroxyterephthalic disodium (HTA), whose fluorescence was measured using spectro-fluorophotometer under excitation of 320 nm and emission at 425 nm (Fig. 3 A). TA is also a well-known selective OH• radical scavenger and it does not react with other radicals such as H2O2, O2 −, HO2 •. As show in the Fig. 3B, high fluorescence in acidic conditions, whereas no fluorescence in neutral pH was detected after the same treatment. In particular, the increased intensity of HTA fluorescence at lower pH values suggested the generation of large number of OH• radicals that can be ascribed to increased Mn release in a more acidic environment and accelerated Fenton-like reaction. These outcome advocate that the LCN's are the preferred carrier of Mn, as they will be non-reactant in neutral normal tissue pH 7.4, but release the Mn ions rapidly due to destabilization of LCN's once located in acidic cancer extracellular environment (pH 6.4) or endosomal/lysosomal pH (pH 5.5). It was attributed to presence of charge converting Mn-oleate (MO) complex which was added initially in the preparation of LCN's nanoparticles. The pH dependent drug release characteristics of LCN@Mn-BA also show that it has a remarkable advantage in terms of radical production that enables endogenous chemotherapy without the need for external stimuli.

Finally, we also compared the Fenton-like catalytic activity of Mn2+ and Fe2+ (Fig. S6). This comparative analysis showed that most favourable conditions for the Fe2+ triggered Fenton-like reaction was the acidic pH (ranging from 1 to 4), and the same catalytic activity was found to be ineffective under pH 5–8. Whereas in contrast to that Mn2+ showed effective catalytic activity in the entire pH range with higher catalytic activity under pH 5. The results of the deactivation of the ferrous catalyst might be due to formation of unreactive ferric complexes. Moreover, this evidences have shown that Mn2+ like Fenton type catalyst can trigger ROS-mediated cell death with high efficiency even in weak acidic conditions adding an advantage to the chemodynamic based anticancer therapy.

In addition to Fenton-like activity LCN@Mn-BA nanoparticles also exhibits fluorescence property. MNPs, prepared by the direct thermolysis of an MO complex with excessive oleate are characterized by the excitation-dependent fluorescence across the entire range of visible spectrum. As illustrated in Fig. 3C, tunable emissions were demonstrated by variation of the wavelength of excitation from 400 nm to 700 nm. This was further confirmed by the fluorescence emissions of MNPs dispersion taken after excitation at various wavelengths.

Further, we have also performed the fluorescence analysis with or without H2O2 at various pH conditions. The findings showed that the fluorescence properties of MNPs were not significantly altered and showed fairly stable fluorescence within a tested pH range with/without H2O2 as shown in the Fig. S7. Since the fluorescence properties of MNPs were mainly due to the presence of a fluorescent carbonaceous layer formed during MNPs preparation, its fluorescent properties was not influenced by the change in pH or by the presence or absence of H2O2.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Drug release and stability properties of LCN@Mn-BA nanoparticles
ce:section.ce:para: We have studied LCN@Mn-BA behavior at different pH values. Ideally, when LCN's are carried from neutral to acidic conditions, the reactor carrier should release its cargo selectively. As shown in Fig. 3D, a clear pH-dependent ionization and good release of Mn2+ ions and BA was observed in the in vitro release study. Higher release rates were observed in pH 5.5 for both BA and Mn compared to pH 6.5 and pH 7.4. At pH 6.4, Mn and BA showed a release rate of 78% and 70% respectively and both showed a release rate of 99% at pH 5.5 in 48 h which was significantly higher than the release rate observed at neutral pH (Mn-30%, BA-25 % release). This release suggests that LCN's are the preferred carrier for the slow release of cargo into normal tissue (neutral pH) and the rapid release of cargo under TME.

Stability of nanoparticles is inextricably linked in order to carry out experiments in vitro or in vivo. So, a six months long-term stability data in PBS (pH 7.4) at 25 °C was performed to determine the long-term physical stability of the LCN's. As shown in Fig. 3F the particle size remain in the range of below 250 nm with a PDI of < 0.4 indicating that the, PF127 could sterically stabilize the liquid crystalline particles.

Similarly, LCN@Mn-BA's serum stability for 24 h was also estimated in the presence of 10% fetal bovine serum (FBS). in serum, the nanoparticles were also found to be stable in terms of particle size and PDI since there was no aggregation and particles remain monodisperse over a period of time (Fig. S8). Our data clearly shows that LCN@Mn-BA are suitable for in-vivo administration.

Evaluation of in vitro hemocompatibility of formulations provides an insight of in vivo compatibility of nanoparticles. We have carried out hemolytic assay using formulation and free drugs containing equivalent concentration of both Mn and BA ranging from 5 to 500 µg/mL. The formulation and free drugs with highest concentration showed in a range of 3.8–4.3% hemolysis in comparison to positive control, while as shown in the Fig. S9, all other formulation showed below to this value. This suggest the safe use of LCN's formulation in vivo experimentation.

ce:section.ce:section-title: In vitro anti-cancer activity of LCN@Mn-BA
ce:section.ce:para: For the in-vitro cell cytotoxicity studies MDA-MB-231 and 4T1 cell lines were used. The basis for selecting MDA-MB-231 cells is that, they are triple negative, aggressive and invasive mammalian breast cancer cells. while 4T1 cells are also very aggressive murine breast cancer cells and cancer growth closely mimic human breast cancer. In addition, the levels of hydrogen peroxide in MDA-MB-231 and 4T1 cells, is higher than in other breast cancer cell lines, such as MCF-7 cell lines [56,57].

A reasonable strategy for amplifying the Mn anti-cancer activity is to deliver this Fenton type catalyst to cancer cells together with other synergetic drug. Therefore BA was loaded into liquid crystalline nanoparticles together with Mn. Initially, cytotoxicity studies on MDA-MB-231 and 4T1 cell lines were carried out using MTT assay over a period of 48 h for both drugs (Mn or BA) individually (Fig. 4 A and S10A). The concentration range determined in previous reports was used as a reference point and then the concentrations Mn-(1–80 μg/mL) and BA-(0.5–20 μg/mL) used in this study were investigated [37,58,59]. The IC50 for BA and Mn was found to be 3.5 μg/mL and 14.4 μg/mL respectively for MDA-MB-231 cell lines and 2.5 μg/mL and 19.5 μg/mL respectively for 4T1 cell lines.

Subsequent to this combination experiment was performed and a few combinations of Mn and BA demonstrated synergism in terms of cytotoxicity than that observed with either drug alone at same concentration i.e. BA and Mn showed 68 ± 5% and 71 ± 4% of cell viability at a concentration of 1.25 μg/mL and at 5 μg/mL respectively in MDA-MB-231 cell lines. While when exposed in combination (BA/Mn-1.25/5 μg/mL) at same concentration the viability was reduced to 40 ± 5%. Further as shown in the Fig. 4B and S10B we analyzed this data in 5 × 6 checker board format using combenefit software, which simultaneously assesses synergy/additive effect using three published models (Highest Single Agent (HSA), Bliss, and Loewe). For each BA and Mn combination the software calculates a synergy score, where a positive value suggest presence of synergism, the score of 0 is additive and the negative score shows antagonism. We used the Bliss model in this combination experiment because it assumes independent mechanisms of action of two drugs. As shown in the Fig. 4B and S10B based on synergy score the modeling data identified efficient synergy between (0.5–2.5 μg/mL) of BA and (2–10 μg/mL) of Mn in both the cell lines.

After identifying synergy combinations, we tried to analyze the same with LCN@Mn-BA formulations loaded with different synergistic drug combinations; BA/Mn-(1.25/5 μg/mL), (1.25/10 μg/mL), (0.5/5 μg/mL) and (0.5/10 μg/mL). As shown in the Fig. 4C and S10C, interestingly LCN@Mn-BA nanoparticles exerted higher cytotoxicity than the Mn+BA cocktail. Cell viability of MDA-MB-231 cells was dropped from 40 ± 5% to 17 ± 4% upon treatment with LCN@Mn-BA and free Mn+BA cocktail containing equivalent concentration of (BA/Mn-1.25/5 μg/mL) respectively. In the similar fashion, LCN@Mn-BA nanoparticles enhanced the cytotoxicity of Mn and BA in 4T1 cell line with the above mentioned synergistic drugs ratio. Results were also investigated using the CompuSyn software for a combination index for drug combination according to the Chou-Talalay model [50]. As shown in Fig. 4D, and S10D cargo loaded in the LCN@Mn-BA nanoparticles maintained the synergy as demonstrated by the Mn+BA cocktail. These observations indicate the better suitability of LCN as a carrier for the better chemotherapeutic effect.

The biosafety of LCN@Mn-BA nanoparticles was also evaluated on normal cells (human embryonic kidney cell line-HEK 293 cells). Interestingly as shown in Fig. S11, at all the drug concentrations tested the viability of HEK-293 cells was found to be more than 94% and LCN@Mn-BA nanoparticles were found to be safe in normal cells. This may be due to the presence of sufficient redox balance in normal cells as compared to cancer cells which possess imbalanced antioxidant activity. This makes the cancer cells more susceptible to ROS damage induced by LCN@Mn-BA.
Finally for the purposes of in vivo and in-vitro studies, we therefore selected safe and selective-toxic synergistic combination (BA/Mn-1.25/5 μg/mL).

ce:section.ce:section-title: In vitro cancer cells imaging using LCN@Mn-BA nanoparticles
ce:section.ce:para: In an exploratory experiment to confirm the potential of LCN@Mn-BA for cell imaging with confocal microscopy, MDA-MB-231 cells and 4T1 cells were cultured in terms of the established protocol. Upon incubation with LCN@Mn-BA at 37 °C for 12 h, MDA-MB-231 cells and 4T1 cells were brightly illuminated by 633 nm laser beam excitation when imaged on the confocal microscope. The LCN@Mn-BA was able to label both the cytoplasm and the nucleus in a notable fashion as shown in Fig. 5 A. The fluorescence of the MDA-MB-231 cells intensified after 1 h, 4 h, 6 h in a time-dependent manner and showed strong fluorescence at 12 h. As shown in the Fig. S12A, similarly the 4T1 cells showed strong fluorescence intensity at 12 h. As a control group, untreated cells were used. LCN@Mn-BA was internalized in breast cancer cells likely through endocytosis, demonstrating the potential of LCN@Mn-BA in cell imaging with confocal microscopy. Furthermore, a quantitative assessment of the uptake of nanoparticles by MBA-MB-231 and 4T1 cells was performed by flow cytometer (Fig. 5 B and C and Fig. S12B). LCN@Mn-BA nanoparticles reported considerably greater fluorescence at 12 h in both cancer cells compared to initial time points. These findings demonstrate the outstanding diagnostic capacity of LCN@Mn-BA nanoparticles through fluorescent property.

ce:section.ce:section-title: LCN@Mn-BA induced Cell cycle arrest and apoptosis in MDA-MB-231 cells
ce:section.ce:para: The results of cell cycle analysis indicated that Mn and BA induced cell cycle arrest in MDA-MB-231 cells. After treatment with free drug and various formulations for 24 h, the phase in which the cell cycle was arrested was analyzed by the cell's DNA content. By estimating the fluorescent propidium iodide (PI) and DNA complex, it was observed that the percentage of cells for the Mn and BA was increased in the G0/G1 phase compared to the control group. The level of G0/G1 phase also increased for the free (Mn+BA) drug cocktail, LCN@Mn and highly elevated for LCN@Mn-BA formulation, which confirmed the LCN@Mn-BA's enhanced antitumor activity. The percentage of cells in each phase of the cell cycle was shown in Fig. 6 A and B.

The effect of LCN@Mn-BA nanoparticles through apoptosis assay on MDA-MB-231 cells was analysed after exposure to a BA/Mn (1.25/5 μg/mL) for 24 h. In a specific phase of a cell cycle, cells with damaged DNA will accumulate and cells with irreversible damage will undergo apoptosis. As shown in Fig. 6C and D after 24 h of LCN@Mn-BA treatment, the percentage of apoptotic cells (52 ± 5%) increased significantly compared to control cells (0 %). Mn+BA cocktail also had an apoptotic effect (38 ± 5%), whereas LCN@Mn-BA nanoparticles had a significant effect on MDA-MB-231 cells within 24 h. This phenomenon is probably due to the fact that the nano drug delivery system enhanced the synergistic action of BA with Mn to escalate the ROS stress in malignant cells which results in the appearance of significant amount of apoptotic cells compared to free drug or untreated cells.

Mitochondrial membrane potential (MMP) is a strategic sign of cell health and its function. Cell dissipates its MMP during early stage of apoptosis. With the help of a cationic dye (JC-1) we tried to detect the changes in mitochondria of MDA-MB-231 cells. The cell-based fluorescence was measured shortly after the follow-up treatment of MDA-MB-231 cells with free drug and LCN formulation. As shown in the Fig. S13 free BA and Mn induced mitochondrial membrane depolarization in 16 ± 3% and 24 ± 3% of the cell population. In combination with the BA+Mn cocktail, the anti-cancer rate increased to 51 ± 5% of the total cells. However, a significant effect has been observed in lyotropic liquid crystalline formulation, 75 ± 6% of cells dissipates its MMP upon LCN@Mn-BA treatment. This results emphasis the importance of LCN formulation over the free drug treatment.

The potential of LCN@Mn-BA liquid crystals to amplify OH• radical havoc in malignant cells was investigated using cell-permeant dichloro-dihydro-fluorescein diacetate (DCFH-DA) dye. DCFH-DA converts in the presence of ROS to a fluorescent product of 2,7-dichlorofluorescent (DCF) which has been measured by the flow cytometer for fluorescence intensity. Results showed that Mn, LCN@Mn increased radical stress in MDA-MB-231 cells in 24 h due to Fenton-like reaction with endogenous H2O2 in MDA-MB-231 cells. As shown in (Fig. 7 A and B), Mn+BA cocktail also induced moderate radical OH• generation indicating BA synergized with Mn to increase the stress of ROS. However, the co-delivery of Mn and BA via LCN carrier induced significantly higher fluorescence intensity than the Mn+BA cocktail testifying to the negative impact of nanoparticles on the viability of cancer cells.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescent analysis for selective detection of OH• free radical
ce:section.ce:para: In this fluorescent test, hydroxyphenyl fluorescein (HPF) was used as a selective OH• indicator. In this chemodynamic approach of cancer therapy, it is necessary to confirm the formation of OH• radical during the occurrence of Fenton-like reaction. HPF is more stable and more specific than DCFDA. HPF shows bright green fluorescence when it gets oxidized. Upon treatment with free drug and LCN formulations, supporting results were obtained to prove the working principle of this investigation. Liquid crystalline formulations have demonstrated an improved OH• generation of Free radicals compared to free drugs (Mn, BA) or its combination, that might be attributed due to increased cellular uptake of LCN@Mn-BA nanoparticles. As shown in the results (Fig. 7C and D) the mean fluorescence intensity (MFI) of LCN@Mn-BA, free Mn and free BA was found to be 210 ± 25, 65 ± 6, 38 ± 5 respectively.

ce:section.ce:section-title: In vivo optical imaging of LCN@Mn-BA
ce:section.ce:para: By the interesting in vitro results, subsequent exploration of the optical imaging property of the LCN's was done on mice models. 4T1 cell-derived xenograft-bearing mouse model was established by subcutaneous injection of 5 × 106 number of 4T1 cells near the mammary glands of mouse. When the tumor volume attained 100 mm3, mice were injected with PBS and LCN@Mn-BA (1 mg/mL MNP equivalent) via tail vein.

The fluorescence images of the mice were recorded at various time points (1 h, 4 h, 8 h, 12 h and 24 h) and LCN@Mn-BA deposition at the mice tumor site was quantified as a region of interest by measuring its fluorescence intensity. Caliper Life Science® IVIS kinetics in vivo optical imaging system with 610 nm excitation and 660 nm emission was used for imaging. As shown in (Fig. 8 A and C), the red fluorescence signal in the tumor area was observed rapidly within 1 h post injection and the intensity of the mean tumor signal increases with time after 4 h, 8 h, and 12 h of post injection and decreased over 24 h. This observation revealed the continued deposition of LCN@Mn-BA nanoparticles in the tumor area, owing to passive targeting or by EPR effect.

The prominent LCN@Mn-BA tumor deposition was also proved using ex-vivo study of excised tumor tissue and other organs. After 24 h of post-injection, mice injected with PBS and LCN@Mn-BA were sacrificed and fluorescence intensities of vital organs such as heart, kidney, spleen, liver, lungs and tumors were properly evaluated to determine the tissue allocation of nanoparticles. The tumor tissue and liver that were removed showed high fluorescence intensity and minimal signals were received from the other organ such as lungs, that might be due to passive accumulation of nanoparticles (Fig. 8B and D).

The above findings notified the amassed property of LCN's at the tumor site while safeguarding the relative constant fluorescence signals after extended systemic circulation throughout the in vivo imaging operation. This allows red emissive LCN@Mn-BA to be used as fluorescent imaging agent in vivo.

ce:section.ce:section-title: In vivo Pharmacokinetics and tumor distribution studies
ce:section.ce:para: The in vivo pharmacokinetics and biodistribution studies were conducted in tumor bearing mice through intravenous administration of different formulations at the equivalent dose of BA (10 mg/kg) and Mn (40 mg/kg). In vivo pharmacokinetic data over a period of 48 h are shown in Fig. 9 A and B and Table 2 . It has been observed that LCN formulation markedly improved the pharmacokinetic profile of both BA and Mn with an increase in AUC and higher t1/2 compared to free drugs. Briefly, upon i.v. administration LCN@Mn-BA showed 10-fold increase in the AUC (66 ± 7 μg/mL h) of BA while 11-fold increase in the AUC (355 ± 18 μg/mL h) of Mn compared to the free BA+Mn physical cocktail. MRT and half-life of LCN@Mn-BA was also prolonged than that of free BA+Mn control cocktail solution. The average half-life (t1/2) of LCN@Mn-BA was 6 fold longer for BA, and 13 fold longer for Mn than free BA+Mn.

The concentration of BA and Mn was not traceable in blood after 8 h when given in the form of BA+Mn physical cocktail, however with LCN@Mn-BA, it remained in the circulation for 48 h with a trough concentration of >100 ng/ml. This clearly indicates that LCN@Mn-BA is able to remain in circulation for prolonged period.

A comparative tumor distribution study has also been conducted to determine the location of the formulations at the tumor site. Fig. 9C and D represent the comparative levels of BA and Mn in the tumor at different time points. Apparently, LCN@Mn-BA has delivered more BA and Mn than a free BA+Mn cocktail. To demonstrate synergistic concentrations at the tumor site, we determined the ratios of BA:Mn at the tumor site. The TME-stimuli sensitive formulation, LCN@Mn-BA, maintained BA to Mn ratios ranging from 6 to 4 which clearly fall within the synergy ratio established by the drug combination experiment in vitro (Fig. 9E).

In order to verify in-vitro intriguing results, LCN@Mn-BA antitumor efficacy in a female BALB/c mouse with 4T1 tumor xenograft was assessed compared to free drugs. 100 µL of free drug solution and nanoparticle dispersion was injected into individual mice intravenously on day 1, 4, 8, 12, and 16 at an equivalent dose of 10 mg/kg of BA and 40 mg/kg of Mn and sacrificed on 21st day of treatment started. On tumor suppression assessments, reasonable effects of tumor regression was observed both in the LCN@Mn-BA and Mn+BA cocktail administrations. In more precise terms, according to tumor volume variations, LCN@Mn-BA's anti-tumor efficacy was 6 fold higher than the Mn+BA cocktail and 29 fold higher than control (Fig. 10 A). The quantitative tumor growth inhibition index (TIX) rates were 97 ± 3 % for LCN@Mn-BA and 75 ± 4% for Mn+BA cocktail compared to control. Furthermore, the TIX rate of LCN@Mn-BA was 22 ± 3 times higher than the treatment group for Mn+BA cocktail (Fig. 10B). During this therapeutic period, control group and all therapeutic groups showed no significant variations in body weight indicating no significant toxicity was induced in mice by injection of LCN@Mn-BA, LCN@Mn nanoparticles or free drugs during therapeutic treatment (Fig. 10C). After administration of LCN@Mn-BA nanoparticles, as shown in Fig. 10D mice tumors were dissected and compared to visually demonstrates effective tumor suppression rate. Survival studies were conducted for 50 days. As shown in Fig. 10E, the median survival of drug cocktail (Mn+BA) was more than free drugs or the control group. In addition, LCN's increased the survival time of the mice even more than the cocktail of free drugs. The medial survival time of control, Mn, BA, Mn+BA, LCN@Mn and LCN@Mn-BA was as follows: 25, 27, 32, 40, >50 days and > 50 days respectively.

Histopathological analysis by H&E staining was performed by collecting organs such as liver, spleen, lung, heart, and tumor at the end of the tumor regression study to assess in vivo tissue toxicity, in particular the potential tissue damage, inflammation, or lesions that LCN@Mn-BA could cause. Fig. 11
presents the representative images of tissue sections of various organs. No major changes was observed in histopathology of vital organs.

Compared to the control group, the tissue structure of the major mice organs administered with a free Mn+BA cocktail, LCN@Mn-BA nanoparticles, was almost intact. No changes in liver tissue such as damage to hepatic lobules, hepatic cords, and pyknotic nuclei formation have been observed. In the spleen, the germinal center was intact suggesting no toxicity to spleen. Cell necrosis or inflammatory infiltrate, damage to glomerular or renal tubular cells, and thickening of alveolar walls have not been observed in histological analysis of heart, kidney and lung tissue respectively.

Whereas in the tumor sections of the control group treated with saline, the cells were dense and regular in shape. While the cancer cells were reduced and the tissue section showed irregular morphologies, and significantly higher and clear apoptosis was observed in the LCN@Mn-BA treated group. It can be inferred from this in vivo tissue toxicity studies, that the TME responsive LCN@Mn-BA nanoparticles are biocompatible which is critically important for biomedical applications in vivo.

In conclusion, we have developed theranostic lyotropic liquid crystalline with chemodynamic approach and fluorescent diagnostic capabilities for the first time in a cancer prevention perspective. Mn2+ generates free OH• radicals in the presence of H2O2 and, unlike Fe2+, catalyzes a Fenton reaction even in mild acid pH. This was confirmed by the detection of free OH• radical generation by HPF and TA-based fluorescence test in a cancer extracellular environment (pH 6.4) and endosomal / lysosomal pH (pH 5.5). In LCN's, MO complex also served as a gateway for drug release. A clear pH-dependent ionization and good release of Mn2+ ions and BA was observed in the in vitro release study. Chemodynamic therapy strategy used in this investigation employs Mn2+ generated reactive oxygen species (ROS) as a weapon to annihilate cancer cells with oxidative stress. Results showed that LCN's not only inhibited the growth of cancerous cell lines (MDA-M-231 and 4T1 cells) but also ensured the safety of normal cells (HEK cells) due to slow drug release and substantially suppressed Fenton reaction in the normal cell microenvironment. BA was loaded into LCN's (LCN@Mn-BA) to synergize the anti-cancer activity of Fenton's reactant-Mn. The cell viability of MDA-MB-231 has been reduced to 40 % for Mn+BA cocktail and 17% for LCN@Mn-BA treated cells compared to control. In addition to having anticancer activity, the LCN@Mn-BA had the ability to diagnose it, MNPs loaded in it possessed the tunable excitation-dependent fluorescence emission over the entire visible spectral region. In cellular uptake studies, this intrinsic fluorescence helped to track nanoparticles without further conjugation or encapsulation of secondary fluorescent nanoprobe. Additionally, LCN@Mn-BA's optical imaging property was used in vivo and ex vivo imaging studies to study nanoparticles distribution in tumor-bearing mice model. In vivo tumor suppression studies on the 4T1 xenograft mice model, a reasonable effect of tumor regression was observed in both LCN@Mn-BA and Mn+BA cocktail treated groups. According to the quantitative tumor growth inhibition index (TIX), LCN@Mn-BA's TIX rate was 97 ± 3% and for Mn+BA cocktail 75 ± 4% as compared to control. Overall, the fluorescent and Fenton-type catalyst loaded LCN's developed in this study could be a comprehensive nano-theranostics for breast cancer bio-imaging and chemodynamic therapy.
